audio.transcript: Today's AI engineering briefing. Item 1: a new speculative-decoding variant. Item 2: a long-context retrieval study. Item 3: a prompt-injection disclosure.

lessons.headline: A faster exact-verification speculative decoder lands in a popular serving stack
summaryBullets: A new draft-model scheme raises the token acceptance rate, so more tokens are verified per target pass.
It uses exact verification, so output stays distributionally identical to the target model.
Reported speedups are largest at low batch sizes, where decode is most bandwidth-bound.
lessons.whyItMatters: Speculative decoding cuts latency without touching quality when verification is exact — but the win shrinks as batch size grows and spare FLOPs disappear. Know your serving regime before adopting it.
lessons.moduleRationale: Directly about speculative decoding (topic 5) and the decode-bandwidth lens (topic 3).

microQuiz.question: Why does exact-verification speculative decoding not change output quality?
options: A rejection-sampling verification step provably reproduces the target model's distribution
The draft model is quantized to match the target
It only runs when the prompt is cached
It averages the draft and target logits
microQuiz.explanation: Exact methods accept/reject draft tokens so the final distribution equals the target's; the draft only affects speed via acceptance rate.

lessons.headline: Study: long context does not replace retrieval for large corpora
summaryBullets: Stuffing more documents into a long window still suffers lost-in-the-middle degradation.
Selective retrieval with reranking beat naive long-context stuffing on grounded QA.
Cost scaled roughly with context length, eroding the convenience argument.
lessons.whyItMatters: Reinforces a core RAG lesson: a bigger window is a budget, not a dumping ground. Curate and rerank rather than paste everything.
lessons.moduleRationale: Retrieval quality and reranking (topics 15-16) plus context curation (topic 2).

microQuiz.question: What is the most likely cause of mid-document accuracy loss when stuffing a long window?
options: Lost-in-the-middle: models attend most to the start and end
The tokenizer corrupts the middle tokens
A hard context limit truncates the middle
microQuiz.explanation: Position bias means key facts buried mid-context are under-attended; rerank to place them at the edges.

lessons.headline: Prompt-injection disclosure: a tool-enabled assistant leaked data via the lethal trifecta
summaryBullets: An assistant with access to private data also ingested attacker-controlled web content.
A link-rendering tool became the exfiltration channel — all three trifecta legs present.
The fix was to break one leg: strip the outbound channel for untrusted-derived content.
lessons.whyItMatters: The lethal trifecta (private data + untrusted content + exfiltration) is the canonical injection failure. Treat the model as untrusted and enforce permissions in your code, not the prompt.
lessons.moduleRationale: Prompt injection and the lethal trifecta (topic 20).

microQuiz.question: What is the most reliable mitigation for the lethal trifecta?
options: Break at least one leg (e.g., remove the exfiltration channel for untrusted-derived actions)
Add a stronger system prompt telling the model to refuse
microQuiz.explanation: Injection defenses in the prompt are bypassable. Remove one leg of data + untrusted content + exfiltration architecturally.
